text:
Building a Rope Kit
Diameter
Crash Restraint says the Core Rope Curriculum generally uses rope between 3/16 and 3/8 inch (5 to 8 millimetres), with 1/4 inch (6 millimetres) a common beginner starting point.
Construction
The page advises against rope with an outer braided sheath around a straight-fiber core, including kernmantle construction, because cored rope is difficult to handle for bondage and produces bulky knots. It recommends either twisted rope, most commonly three-strand but sometimes four-strand, or a solid braid with no core.
Color
The page advises beginners to avoid black or very dark rope because it is harder for the learner or teacher to see what is happening.
Length
It recommends building a kit mainly from the longest rope the user can comfortably handle, plus several shorter ropes for finishing ties.
Material and other equipment
For beginners who do not yet know their preferences, the page suggests inexpensive, high-quality three-strand cotton or nylon. It identifies safety cutters as the most important non-rope item in a rope bag and also discusses water, a sugary snack, basic first aid, a clean floor covering, and access to any needed emergency medication.
title: Building a Rope Kit
